title: Ο περί Ασφάλειας Συστημάτων Δικτύου και Πληροφοριών Νόμος
title_en: Law on the Security of Network and Information Systems
description: The Cypriot law transposing Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (NIS2 Directive) on measures for a high common level of cybersecurity across the Union. The Law establishes the Digital Security Authority as the national competent authority, defines essential and important entities, imposes cybersecurity risk management measures and incident reporting obligations, and establishes a national cybersecurity strategy framework. It repeals and replaces the earlier NIS1 transposition.

provisions.title: Article 1. Object and Scope
provisions.content: This Law lays down measures with a view to achieving a high common level of cybersecurity within the Republic of Cyprus, with a view to improving the functioning of the internal market. To that end, this Law: (a) lays down obligations requiring Member States to adopt national cybersecurity strategies; (b) establishes the Digital Security Authority (Αρχή Ψηφιακής Ασφάλειας) as the national competent authority responsible for cybersecurity and as the national single point of contact, and designates a Computer Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT); (c) lays down cybersecurity risk-management measures and reporting obligations for entities of a type referred to in Annexes I and II and for entities identified as essential or important entities under Article 3 of this Law; (d) lays down cybersecurity risk-management measures and reporting obligations for DNS service providers, TLD name registries, entities providing domain name registration services, cloud computing service providers, data centre service providers, content delivery network providers, managed service providers, managed security service providers, online market places, online search engines, social networking services platforms, and trust service providers. This Law implements Directive (EU) 2022/2555.

provisions.title: Article 2. Definitions
provisions.content: For the purposes of this Law: 'network and information system' means (a) an electronic communications network as defined in Directive (EU) 2018/1972; (b) any device or group of interconnected or related devices, one or more of which, pursuant to a program, automatically processes digital data; or (c) digital data stored, processed, retrieved or transmitted by elements covered under points (a) and (b) for the purposes of their operation, use, protection and maintenance; 'security of network and information systems' means the ability of network and information systems to resist, at a given level of confidence, any action that compromises the availability, authenticity, integrity or confidentiality of stored, transmitted or processed data or of the services offered by, or accessible via, those network and information systems; 'cybersecurity incident' or 'incident' means an event that compromises the availability, authenticity, integrity or confidentiality of stored, transmitted or processed data or of the services offered by, or accessible via, network and information systems; 'essential entity' means an entity referred to in Article 3(1); 'important entity' means an entity referred to in Article 3(2).

provisions.title: Article 3. Essential and Important Entities
provisions.content: For the purposes of this Law, the following entities shall be deemed essential entities: (a) entities of a type referred to in Annex I that exceed the ceilings for medium-sized enterprises within the meaning of Article 2 of the Annex to Commission Recommendation 2003/361/EC; (b) qualified trust service providers and TLD name registries and DNS service providers, regardless of their size; (c) entities that have been identified as critical entities under Council Directive 2008/114/EC; (d) entities for which the Digital Security Authority has determined, on the basis of a risk assessment, that they are of critical importance and should be treated as essential entities. For the purposes of this Law, the following entities shall be deemed important entities: (a) entities of a type referred to in Annex I that are medium-sized enterprises; (b) entities of a type referred to in Annex II that exceed the ceilings for medium-sized enterprises; (c) entities that the Digital Security Authority has determined should be treated as important entities. The Digital Security Authority shall establish and maintain a register of essential and important entities and shall review and update the register at least every two years.

provisions.title: Article 4. Digital Security Authority
provisions.content: The Digital Security Authority (DSA) is established as the national competent authority for cybersecurity under this Law. The DSA shall be responsible for: (a) overseeing the application of this Law at national level; (b) acting as a single point of contact on cybersecurity for purposes of cross-border cooperation with competent authorities of other Member States; (c) designating and overseeing the national CSIRT; (d) coordinating the handling of large-scale cybersecurity incidents and crises at national level; (e) reporting on national cybersecurity status to the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) and to the NIS Cooperation Group. The DSA shall have the power to conduct inspections and audits of essential and important entities, to request information and documentation, and to issue binding instructions to entities to remedy identified deficiencies within a specified period. The DSA shall cooperate with the supervisory authorities for the financial sector, including the Central Bank of Cyprus, in matters of overlapping competence.

provisions.title: Article 5. National Cybersecurity Strategy
provisions.content: The Council of Ministers shall adopt a national cybersecurity strategy that sets out the strategic objectives, resources and policy and regulatory measures, with a view to achieving and maintaining a high level of cybersecurity. The national cybersecurity strategy shall address at least the following: (a) the objectives and priorities of the Republic of Cyprus's cybersecurity strategy, covering in particular the sectors referred to in Annexes I and II; (b) a governance framework to achieve the objectives and priorities of the national cybersecurity strategy, including the policies and the regulatory measures referred to in points (c) to (j); (c) a framework addressing cybersecurity in supply chains for ICT products and ICT services used by entities for the provision of their services; (d) measures to achieve a high level of security awareness and cyber hygiene among citizens, including guidance for citizens and organisations on individual online security practices, cyber hygiene and digital literacy; (e) a policy regarding the active promotion of and facilitation of the coordinated disclosure of vulnerabilities pursuant to Article 12 of Directive (EU) 2022/2555. The national cybersecurity strategy shall be reviewed at least every five years.

provisions.title: Article 6. Cybersecurity Risk-Management Measures
provisions.content: Essential and important entities shall take appropriate and proportionate technical, operational and organisational measures to manage the risks posed to the security of network and information systems which those entities use for their operations or for the provision of their services, and to prevent or minimise the impact of incidents on recipients of their services and on other services. The measures shall ensure a level of security of network and information systems appropriate to the risks posed, taking into account the state of the art, and, as appropriate, the relevant European and international standards, as well as the cost of implementation. The measures shall be based on an all-hazards approach that aims to protect network and information systems and the physical environment of those systems from incidents. The measures shall include at least: (a) policies on risk analysis and information system security; (b) incident handling; (c) business continuity, such as backup management and disaster recovery, and crisis management; (d) supply chain security, including security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers; (e) security in network and information systems acquisition, development and maintenance, including vulnerability handling and disclosure; (f) policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures; (g) basic cyber hygiene practices and cybersecurity training; (h) policies and procedures regarding the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption; (i) human resources security, access control policies and asset management; (j) the use of multi-factor authentication or continuous authentication solutions.

provisions.title: Article 7. Incident Reporting Obligations
provisions.content: Essential and important entities shall notify the CSIRT or, where applicable, the competent authority without undue delay: (a) an early warning, without undue delay and in any event within 24 hours of becoming aware of a significant incident, that includes, where applicable, an indication of whether the significant incident is suspected of being caused by unlawful or malicious acts and whether it is likely to have a cross-border impact; (b) an incident notification, without undue delay and in any event within 72 hours of becoming aware of the significant incident, which shall, where applicable, update the information referred to in point (a) and include an initial assessment of the significant incident, including its severity and impact, as well as indicators of compromise, where available; (c) an intermediate report, if requested by the CSIRT or, where applicable, the competent authority; (d) a final report no later than one month after the submission of the incident notification referred to in point (b), including: (i) a detailed description of the incident, including its severity and impact; (ii) the type of threat or root cause that is likely to have triggered the incident; (iii) applied and ongoing mitigation measures; (iv) where applicable, the cross-border impact of the incident. A significant incident shall be considered to exist where the incident has caused or is capable of causing severe operational disruption of the services or financial loss for the entity concerned, or has affected or is capable of affecting other natural or legal persons by causing considerable material or non-material losses.

provisions.title: Article 8. Computer Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT)
provisions.content: The Digital Security Authority shall designate a Computer Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT) responsible for risk and incident handling in accordance with a well-defined process. The CSIRT shall have adequate resources and technical capabilities to carry out its tasks and shall comply with the requirements set out in Article 11(1) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555. The CSIRT's tasks shall include: (a) monitoring and analysing cyber threats, vulnerabilities and incidents at national level; (b) providing early warning, alerts, announcements and dissemination of information to relevant stakeholders about cyber threats, vulnerabilities and incidents; (c) responding to incidents and providing assistance to the entities concerned; (d) collecting and analysing forensic data and providing dynamic risk and incident analysis and situational awareness regarding cybersecurity; (e) identifying and promoting the adoption of common practices for incident and crisis management, and common classification schemes with respect to cybersecurity incidents, cyber threats and vulnerabilities; (f) coordinating the coordinated disclosure of vulnerabilities; (g) contributing to the deployment of secure information-sharing tools at national level.

provisions.title: Article 9. Supervisory and Enforcement Measures
provisions.content: In order to carry out the supervisory tasks referred to in this Law, the DSA shall have the following powers with respect to essential entities: (a) on-site inspections and off-site supervision, including random checks carried out by trained professionals; (b) regular and targeted security audits carried out by an independent body or a competent authority; (c) ad hoc audits, including those justified by a significant incident or a breach of this Law by the essential entity; (d) security scans based on objective, non-discriminatory, fair and transparent risk assessment criteria; (e) requests for information necessary to assess the cybersecurity risk-management measures adopted by the entity; (f) requests for access to data, documents and any other information necessary for the performance of the supervisory tasks; (g) requests for evidence of implementation of cybersecurity policies. Where the DSA determines that an essential entity has failed to comply with its obligations under this Law, the DSA may: (i) issue a warning; (ii) issue a binding instruction; (iii) impose a temporary ban on the performance of management functions by the entity's management body; (iv) impose administrative fines.

provisions.title: Article 10. Administrative Fines
provisions.content: Essential entities that fail to comply with the obligations set out in Articles 6 and 7 of this Law shall be subject to administrative fines of up to €10,000,000 or 2% of total worldwide annual turnover in the preceding financial year, whichever amount is higher. Important entities that fail to comply with the obligations set out in Articles 6 and 7 of this Law shall be subject to administrative fines of up to €7,000,000 or 1.4% of total worldwide annual turnover in the preceding financial year, whichever amount is higher. When determining whether to impose an administrative fine and its amount, the DSA shall take into account: (a) the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement; (b) the scope of the infringement and the number of persons affected; (c) any action taken by the entity to prevent or mitigate the damage; (d) whether the infringement was intentional or negligent; (e) any previous infringements by the entity; (f) the degree of cooperation with the DSA; (g) the financial situation of the entity. Administrative fines shall be effective, proportionate and dissuasive.

provisions.title: Article 11. Supply Chain Security
provisions.content: Essential and important entities shall address cybersecurity risks in supply chains, including security-related aspects concerning the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers. When assessing the risks in supply chain security, entities shall take into account: (a) the specific risks posed by suppliers or service providers, in particular those providing ICT products or ICT services; (b) the overall quality and resilience of the products and services; (c) the cybersecurity practices of suppliers and service providers. The DSA, in cooperation with the NIS Cooperation Group and ENISA, may issue guidelines on the factors to be taken into account in assessing the cybersecurity risk of specific ICT products or services, including specific criteria for assessing whether a supplier or service provider represents a high-risk supplier. Where the Council of the European Union has adopted an implementing act identifying ICT products, ICT services or ICT processes covered by a European cybersecurity certification scheme as defined in Regulation (EU) 2019/881, the DSA may require essential entities to use certified ICT products, ICT services or ICT processes within the relevant category.

provisions.title: Article 12. Sector-Specific Cybersecurity Provisions
provisions.content: The sectors designated as critical for the purposes of this Law are listed in Annexes I and II. Annex I lists highly critical sectors including: energy (electricity, district heating and cooling, oil, gas, hydrogen); transport (air, rail, water, road); banking; financial market infrastructure; health including healthcare providers, EU reference laboratories and manufacturers of medicines; drinking water; waste water; digital infrastructure including internet exchange points, DNS service providers, TLD name registries, cloud computing service providers, data centre service providers, content delivery network providers, trust service providers, providers of public electronic communications networks or of publicly available electronic communications services; ICT service management (managed service providers); public administration; and space. Annex II lists other critical sectors including postal and courier services, waste management, manufacture production and distribution of chemicals, food production processing and distribution, manufacturing of medical devices, computers and electronics, machinery, motor vehicles and other transport equipment, digital providers including online marketplaces, online search engines, and social networking services platforms, and research.

provisions.title: Article 13. Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure
provisions.content: The DSA shall facilitate the coordinated disclosure of vulnerabilities by designating a coordinating CSIRT for the coordinated disclosure of vulnerabilities. The CSIRT shall act as a trusted intermediary between the reporting natural or legal person and the manufacturer or provider of the potentially vulnerable ICT products or ICT services, where necessary, facilitating the interaction between the reporting natural or legal person and the manufacturer or provider of the potentially vulnerable ICT products or ICT services; the CSIRT may also invite other relevant CSIRTs, researchers or industry bodies to participate in the coordinated disclosure process. The DSA shall establish and maintain a national vulnerability registry containing information about known vulnerabilities in ICT products and ICT services. The DSA shall contribute information to ENISA's European vulnerability database established under Article 12(2) of Directive (EU) 2022/2555.

provisions.title: Article 14. Peer Reviews
provisions.content: The Republic of Cyprus, acting through the DSA, may request and shall participate in peer reviews organised by the NIS Cooperation Group. Peer reviews shall be carried out by cybersecurity experts from Member States other than the Member State under review. Peer reviews may cover: (a) the level of implementation of the cybersecurity risk-management measures and reporting obligations; (b) the level of capabilities, including available financial, technical and human resources, and the effectiveness of the performance of tasks of the competent authorities; (c) the technical capabilities and available resources of CSIRTs; (d) the level of implementation of mutual assistance provisions; (e) the level of implementation of the cybersecurity information-sharing arrangements. Within three months of completion of the peer review, the DSA shall publish a report setting out the outcome of the peer review and any recommendations made for improvement, unless the publication would jeopardise the security of network and information systems.

provisions.title: Article 15. Information Sharing
provisions.content: Entities covered by this Law may voluntarily share cybersecurity information with each other and with the DSA, where such sharing: (a) aims to prevent incidents or support the detection, analysis, response to or recovery from incidents; (b) enhances the level of cybersecurity, in particular through sharing of threat intelligence including information on threats, vulnerabilities, indicators of compromise, tactics, techniques and procedures, and cybersecurity alerts. The DSA shall facilitate the establishment of sector-specific information sharing arrangements covering at least the energy, transport, financial, health, and digital infrastructure sectors. Information shared within such arrangements shall be subject to appropriate confidentiality obligations. The sharing of information on vulnerabilities with the DSA or with other entities under this Article shall not constitute grounds for imposing additional regulatory liability on the entity sharing such information. The DSA shall provide guidelines on secure methods for information sharing.

definitions.definition: An electronic communications network, any device or group of interconnected or related devices, one or more of which automatically processes digital data pursuant to a program, or digital data stored, processed, retrieved or transmitted by such elements.

An event that compromises the availability, authenticity, integrity or confidentiality of stored, transmitted or processed data or of the services offered by, or accessible via, network and information systems.

An entity that exceeds the ceilings for medium-sized enterprises operating in a highly critical sector listed in Annex I, or an entity specifically designated as essential by the Digital Security Authority on the basis of a risk assessment.
